reforms.description: The government plans to broaden the contestable funding pools to include all players, particularly government labs, to enhance competition and innovation in research funding. This initiative is part of the priority recommendations developed in the November 2002 National Summit on the Innovation Strategy to enhance Canada's innovation performance.
reforms.growth_orientation_rationale: Enhancing competition in research funding is expected to stimulate innovation and improve overall research outcomes.
reforms.status_evidence: The reform is currently announced and not yet implemented.
reforms.importance_rationale: The reform broadens funding access, which is significant but not a complete overhaul of the funding system.
reforms.source_quote: Quickly implement the priority recommendations developed in the November 2002 National Summit on the Innovation Strategy.

reforms.description: The Canadian government aims to simplify the application process for the R&D tax credit to encourage more businesses to engage in research and development activities. This is part of the broader Innovation Strategy launched in February 2002, which seeks to close the innovation gap with the United States by enhancing knowledge commercialization and strengthening the workforce's skill levels.
reforms.growth_orientation_rationale: Simplifying the R&D tax credit application process is expected to incentivize more businesses to invest in R&D, thereby fostering innovation.
reforms.status_evidence: The reform has been implemented as part of the Innovation Strategy.
reforms.importance_rationale: This reform represents a significant change in the governance of innovation support through tax incentives.
reforms.source_quote: The government launched Canada’s Innovation Strategy, which sets out long-term goals for creating a strong framework for innovation and to improving work-related skill levels.

reforms.description: Canada's Innovation Strategy emphasizes the importance of raising skill levels in the workforce to enhance long-term growth and reduce unemployment. The Canada Graduate Scholarship Programme was introduced to provide 4,000 scholarships each year at the Masters and Doctoral level, alongside increased funding for the science sector and university research.
reforms.growth_orientation_rationale: Investing in human capital through scholarships and funding for education is expected to enhance the skill level of the workforce, contributing to long-term economic growth.
reforms.status_evidence: The scholarship programme and funding increases have been implemented as part of the strategy.
reforms.importance_rationale: While significant, these reforms are part of a broader strategy rather than a standalone major reform.
reforms.source_quote: Raising skill levels of the workforce is a key part of Canada’s Innovation Strategy and is important not only for increasing the long-term growth rate but also for getting the unemployed back to work.

reforms.description: The Innovation Strategy includes a recommendation to improve the relationship between the receptor community and universities, colleges, and researchers, aiming to strengthen receptor capacity and incentivize the private sector to commercialize new products.
reforms.growth_orientation_rationale: Improving collaboration between academia and the private sector is expected to enhance commercialization efforts and drive innovation.
reforms.status_evidence: The recommendation has been implemented as part of the Innovation Strategy.
reforms.importance_rationale: This reform is significant in enhancing knowledge transfer but does not represent a major overhaul of the system.
reforms.source_quote: Improve the relationship between the receptor community and universities, colleges and researchers.
